title.zh: 卷四十一 第五鍾離宋寒列傳
title.en: Volume 41: Biographies of Diwu, Zhongli, Song, Han
title.raw: 卷四十一 第五鍾離宋寒列傳 Volume 41: Biographies of Diwu, Zhongli, Song, Han

sentences.zh: 第五倫

translations.literal: Diwu Lun.
translations.idiomatic: Diwu Lun

sentences.zh: 第五倫字伯魚，京兆長陵人也。

translations.literal: Diwu Lun, styled Boyu, was a man of Changling in Jingzhao.
translations.idiomatic: Diwu Lun, courtesy name Boyu, came from Changling in the Jingzhao commandery.

sentences.zh: 其先齊諸田，諸田徙園陵者多，故以次第為氏。

translations.literal: His ancestors were among the Tian lineages of Qi; many Tian who were relocated to the imperial mausoleum districts took their surname according to ordinal rank.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears belonged to the Tian families of Qi; when many of those clans were moved into the tomb parklands, they took surnames from their place on the roster—his branch became "Diwu," the Fifth.

sentences.zh: 倫少介然有義行。

translations.literal: In his youth Lun was upright by nature and showed righteous conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Even as a young man he was stiffly principled and acted with a strong sense of right and wrong.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，盜賊起，宗族閭里爭往附之。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang's time, bandits arose, and his clan and village neighbors vied to go and attach themselves to him.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang fell and rebellion spread, his kinsmen and neighbors rushed to rally under his banner.

sentences.zh: 倫乃依險固築營壁，有賊，輒奮厲其眾，引強持滿以拒之，銅馬、赤眉之屬前後數十輩，皆不能下。

translations.literal: Lun then took advantage of rugged terrain and built ramparts; whenever there were bandits, he would rouse his followers, draw bows to the full, and resist them. The Bronze Horse, Red Eyebrows, and the like—several tens of bands in succession—none could overcome him.
translations.idiomatic: He threw up fortified camps on defensible ground. Each time raiders came he rallied his men, put every bowstring to the ear, and threw them back. Dozens of bands over the years—including the Bronze Horse and Red Eyebrow armies—failed to break him.

sentences.zh: 倫始以營長詣郡尹鮮于褒，褒見而異之，署為吏。

translations.literal: Lun first went as camp chief to the prefect Xianyu Bao; Bao saw him and considered him unusual, and appointed him as a clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He first reported to Prefect Xianyu Bao as leader of the camp. Bao was impressed and gave him a post on the official staff.

sentences.zh: 後褒坐事左轉高唐令，臨去，握倫臂訣曰：「恨相知晚。」

translations.literal: Later Bao was implicated in an affair and was transferred downward to be magistrate of Gaotang; on the point of departure he grasped Lun's arm in farewell and said, "I regret that we came to know each other late."
translations.idiomatic: Later Bao was caught up in some matter and was demoted to magistrate of Gaotang. As he left he took Lun's arm in parting and said, "If only we had met sooner."

sentences.zh: 倫後為鄉嗇夫，平徭賦，理怨結，得人歡心。

translations.literal: Lun later became village overseer, equalized labor service and tax levies, sorted out accumulated grievances, and won the people's favor.
translations.idiomatic: He went on to serve as village overseer, balancing corvée and taxes, untangling old quarrels, and earning genuine goodwill.

sentences.zh: 自以為久宦不達，遂將家屬客河東，變名姓，自稱王伯齊，載鹽往來太原、上黨，所過輒為糞除而去，陌上號為道士，親友故人莫知其處。

translations.literal: Thinking himself long in office without advancement, he led his family as guests in Hedong, changed his name, called himself Wang Boqi, carried salt back and forth between Taiyuan and Shangdang, wherever he passed would sweep out dung and depart; those on the roads called him a Daoist adept, and relatives and old friends did not know where he was.
translations.idiomatic: Believing he would never rise after years in minor posts, he moved his household to sojourn in Hedong under an assumed name, calling himself Wang Boqi. He hauled salt between Taiyuan and Shangdang, and everywhere he went he cleaned up manure from the road as he passed—so travelers took him for a wandering ascetic. Friends and family lost track of him entirely.

sentences.zh: 數年，鮮于褒薦之於京兆尹閻興，興即召倫為主簿。

translations.literal: After several years, Xianyu Bao recommended him to the governor of Jingzhao, Yan Xing, and Xing immediately summoned Lun to be his chief clerk.
translations.idiomatic: A few years later Xianyu Bao recommended him to Yan Xing, governor of Jingzhao, who at once appointed him chief clerk.

sentences.zh: 時長安鑄錢多奸巧，乃署倫為督鑄錢掾，領長安市。

translations.literal: At that time Chang'an coin casting involved much fraud, so he appointed Lun as supervisor of minting and put him in charge of the Chang'an market.
translations.idiomatic: Chang'an's mints were riddled with cheating, so Lun was named superintendent of coinage and given charge of the capital market.

sentences.zh: 倫平銓衡，正斗斛，市無阿枉，百姓悅服。

translations.literal: Lun balanced the scales, corrected the pecks and bushels; the market had no crooked partiality, and the hundred surnames were pleased and submitted.
translations.idiomatic: He standardized weights and measures and straightened out the bushel and peck. The market ran fair—no favoritism—and the people trusted him.

sentences.zh: 每讀詔書，常歎息曰：「此聖主也，一見決矣。

translations.literal: Whenever he read an edict, he would often sigh and say, "This is a sage ruler; one audience would decide it.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he read an imperial rescript he would sigh and say, "Here is a true sage-king—one meeting with him would settle everything."

sentences.zh: 」等輩笑之曰：「爾說將尚不下，安能動萬乘乎？

translations.literal: " His peers laughed at him and said, "You cannot even persuade a general; how can you move the ruler of ten thousand chariots?
translations.idiomatic: " His companions jeered: "You cannot win over a mere commander—how would you ever sway the Son of Heaven?"

sentences.zh: 」倫曰：「未遇知己，道不同故耳。」

translations.literal: " Lun said, "I have not yet met one who knows me; it is because our ways are not the same."
translations.idiomatic: " Lun answered, "I have not yet met the patron who would understand me—that is all. Our paths simply did not cross."

sentences.zh: 建武二十七年，舉孝廉，補淮陽國醫工長，隨王之國。

translations.literal: In the twenty-seventh year of Jianwu he was recommended as filial and incorrupt, was appointed senior physician to the principality of Huaiyang, and accompanied the king to his state.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 27 he was recommended on the filial-and-incorrupt list and posted chief physician to the Huaiyang principality, accompanying the prince to his fief.

sentences.zh: 光武召見，甚異之。

translations.literal: Guangwu summoned him for audience and considered him very extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu received him in audience and was deeply impressed.

sentences.zh: 二十九年，從王朝京師，隨官屬得會見，帝問以政事，倫因此酬對政道，帝大悅。

translations.literal: In the twenty-ninth year, he followed the king to the capital and, accompanying the officials, obtained an audience; the emperor questioned him on government affairs, and Lun thereupon responded on the principles of rule; the emperor was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-ninth year he came to the capital with his prince and, joining the princely staff, gained an audience. The emperor questioned him on statecraft; Lun answered cogently on policy, and the sovereign was delighted.

sentences.zh: 明日，復特召入，與語至夕。

translations.literal: The next day he was again specially summoned to enter, and they spoke until evening.
translations.idiomatic: The following day he was summoned again and talked with the emperor until nightfall.

sentences.zh: 帝戲謂倫曰：「聞卿為吏篣婦公，不過從兄飯，寧有之邪？

translations.literal: The emperor jokingly said to Lun, "I have heard that as an official you beat your wife's father and would eat only at your elder cousin's—is there such a thing?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor teased him: "They say you flogged your father-in-law when you held office and took meals only at your cousin's table—any truth to that?"

sentences.zh: 」倫對曰：「臣三娶妻皆無父。

translations.literal: " Lun replied, "Your servant has thrice taken wives, and all were without fathers.
translations.idiomatic: " Lun replied, "I have married three times, and none of my wives' fathers was still living."

sentences.zh: 少遭饑亂，實不敢妄過人食。

translations.literal: In youth I encountered famine and turmoil, and truly did not dare rashly to pass others' food.
translations.idiomatic: I grew up in famine and chaos and never dared impose on others for meals."

sentences.zh: 」帝大笑。

translations.literal: " The emperor laughed greatly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor roared with laughter.

sentences.zh: 倫出，有詔以為扶夷長，未到官，追拜會稽太守。

translations.literal: When Lun went out, there was an edict appointing him magistrate of Fuyi; before he reached his post, he was promoted by supplemental appointment to governor of Kuaiji.
translations.idiomatic: As he withdrew, an edict named him magistrate of Fuyi, but before he could take up the post a follow-up order elevated him to governor of Kuaiji.

sentences.zh: 雖為二千石，躬自斬芻養馬，妻執炊爨。

translations.literal: Although he held rank of two thousand piculs, he personally cut fodder and tended horses, and his wife handled cooking and fires.
translations.idiomatic: Though he held a governor's salary, he cut hay and groomed his own horses while his wife cooked the meals.

sentences.zh: 受俸裁留一月糧，餘皆賤貿與民之貧羸者。

translations.literal: Of the salary he received he kept only one month's grain; the rest he cheaply sold and traded to the people who were poor and weak.
translations.idiomatic: He kept only one month's grain from his pay and sold the rest at low prices to the poorest of his people.

sentences.zh: 會稽俗多淫祀，好卜筮。

translations.literal: Kuaiji custom had many excessive sacrifices and loved divination.
translations.idiomatic: Kuaiji was given to lavish, illegitimate shrines and to fortune-telling.

sentences.zh: 民常以牛祭神，百姓財產以之困匱，其自食牛肉而不以薦祠者，發病且死先為牛鳴，前後郡將莫敢禁。

translations.literal: The people constantly used oxen to sacrifice to spirits; the hundred surnames' wealth became exhausted thereby. Those who ate beef themselves and did not offer it at sacrifice would fall ill and, before dying, would first make ox sounds; successive prefects did not dare prohibit it.
translations.idiomatic: People routinely slaughtered oxen for unauthorized cults until households were bled dry. Anyone who ate beef without dedicating it to a shrine was said to fall ill and, before death, to low like a cow—yet one governor after another had feared to ban the practice.

sentences.zh: 倫到宮，移書屬縣，曉告百姓。

translations.literal: When Lun arrived at his office, he sent documents to the subordinate counties and clearly informed the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: When he took up his post he circularized every county under his command and published clear rules for the people.

sentences.zh: 其巫祝有依托鬼神詐怖愚民，皆案論之。

translations.literal: As for shamans and invokers who relied on ghosts and spirits to deceive and frighten the foolish people, all were investigated and sentenced.
translations.idiomatic: Shamans who invented spirit possession to terrify the credulous were arrested and prosecuted.

sentences.zh: 有妄屠牛者，吏輒行罰。

translations.literal: Those who wantonly slaughtered oxen, officials immediately carried out punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who butchered cattle unlawfully was fined or punished on the spot.

sentences.zh: 民初頗恐懼，或祝詛妄言，倫案之愈急，後遂斷絕，百姓以安。

translations.literal: At first the people were rather fearful; some cursed and spoke wildly; Lun investigated them more urgently, and afterward it was cut off, and the hundred surnames were thereby secure.
translations.idiomatic: At first the populace was frightened, and a few muttered curses; Lun pressed prosecutions harder until the abuses stopped and calm returned.

sentences.zh: 永平五年，坐法征，老小攀車叩馬，啼呼相隨，日裁行數里，不得前，倫乃偽止亭舍，陰乘船去。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongping, he was summoned on account of law; old and young clung to the cart and beat the horses, weeping and calling as they followed; in a day they could advance only several li and could not go forward; Lun then falsely stopped at a post station and secretly took a boat and departed.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 5 he was recalled to answer a legal charge. Young and old clung to his carriage and horses, wailing as they followed; his escort could cover only a few miles a day. Lun pretended to halt at a relay inn, then slipped away by boat.

sentences.zh: 眾知，復追之。

translations.literal: When the crowd knew, they again pursued him.
translations.idiomatic: When the crowd realized what he had done, they set off after him again.

sentences.zh: 及詣廷尉，吏民上書守闕者千餘人。

translations.literal: When he arrived at the commandant of justice, officials and people who submitted memorials guarding the palace gate were more than a thousand.
translations.idiomatic: By the time he reached the court of the commandant of justice, over a thousand clerks and commoners had petitioned at the palace gates on his behalf.

sentences.zh: 是時，顯宗方案梁松事，亦多為松訟者。

translations.literal: At this time the Manifest Emperor was just investigating Liang Song's affair, and there were also many who pled for Song.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment Emperor Ming was hearing the case against Liang Song, and many others were pleading for Song as well.

sentences.zh: 帝患之，詔公車諸為梁氏及會稽太守上書者勿復受。

translations.literal: The emperor was troubled by it and ordered the public carriage office no longer to accept those who submitted memorials for the Liang clan and for the governor of Kuaiji.
translations.idiomatic: Annoyed, the emperor ordered the petition office to refuse any more memorials on behalf of the Liangs or the governor of Kuaiji.

sentences.zh: 會帝幸廷尉錄囚徒，得免歸田里。

translations.literal: It happened that the emperor visited the commandant of justice to review prisoners, and he obtained release to return to fields and village.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor inspected the prison rolls at the commandant's court, Lun was cleared and sent home.

sentences.zh: 身自耕種，不交通人物。

translations.literal: He personally plowed and planted and did not associate with people.
translations.idiomatic: He farmed his own land and kept aloof from public life.

sentences.zh: 數歲，拜為宕渠令，顯拔鄉佐玄賀，賀後為九江、沛二郡守，以清潔稱，所在化行，終於大司農。

translations.literal: After several years he was appointed magistrate of Ququ; he prominently elevated the village aide Xuan He; He later became governor of the two commanderies Jiujiang and Pei, was praised for purity, wherever he was transformation was practiced, and ended as grand minister of agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: A few years later he was named magistrate of Ququ, where he singled out the village aide Xuan He for promotion. He went on to govern Jiujiang and Pei, earning renown for incorruption; good order followed him everywhere, and he rose to finish as grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 倫在職四年，遷蜀郡太守。

translations.literal: Lun held office four years and was transferred to be governor of Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: After four years in office he was promoted to governor of Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 蜀地肥饒，人吏富實，掾史家資多至千萬，皆鮮車怒馬，以財貨自達。

translations.literal: Shu land was fertile and rich; people and officials were wealthy and substantial; clerks' family assets often reached millions; all had bright carriages and spirited horses and made themselves known through wealth and goods.
translations.idiomatic: Shu was rich country; officials and gentry flaunted fortunes in the millions, drove glossy carriages behind spirited horses, and bought influence with money.

sentences.zh: 倫悉簡其豐贍者遣還之，更選孤貧志行之人以處曹任，於是爭賕抑絕，文職修理。

translations.literal: Lun selected out all the abundantly wealthy ones and sent them back, and anew chose orphaned, poor, and principled men to fill bureau duties; thereby competing bribes were suppressed and cut off, and civil offices were put in order.
translations.idiomatic: Lun sent every clerk who lived in luxury packing and replaced them with poor but honest men. Bribery dried up and the paperwork ran straight.

sentences.zh: 所舉吏多至九卿、二千石，時以為知人。

translations.literal: Of those clerks he elevated, many reached the nine ministers and two thousand piculs; at the time he was thought to know men.
translations.idiomatic: Many of his picks rose to ministerial rank and governorships; contemporaries said he had a gift for reading character.

sentences.zh: 視事七歲，肅宗初立，擢自遠郡，代牟融為司空。

translations.literal: After seven years in office, when Emperor Zhang first ascended, he was promoted from a distant commandery and replaced Mou Rong as minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years into his tenure Emperor Zhang came to the throne and plucked him from a remote post to succeed Mou Rong as minister of works.

sentences.zh: 帝以明德太后故，尊崇舅氏馬廖，兄弟並居職任。

translations.literal: Because of Empress Dowager Mingde, the emperor honored his maternal uncle Ma Liao; the brothers together held official posts.
translations.idiomatic: Out of respect for Empress Dowager Mingde, the emperor favored her brother Ma Liao and his siblings with high office.

sentences.zh: 廖等傾身交結，冠蓋之士爭赴趣之。

translations.literal: Liao and the others threw themselves into associations; capped-coach gentlemen vied to hurry to them.
translations.idiomatic: The Ma brothers poured energy into building connections, and every ambitious gentleman in cap and carriage scrambled to court them.

sentences.zh: 倫以後族過盛，欲令朝廷抑損其權，上疏曰：

translations.literal: Lun thought the empress's clan was excessively flourishing and wished the court to suppress and diminish their power; he submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Believing the empress's kin had grown too strong, Lun urged the court to rein them in and submitted a memorial that began:

sentences.zh: 及馬防為車騎將軍，當出征西羌，倫又上疏曰：

translations.literal: When Ma Fang became general of chariots and cavalry and was about to go out on campaign against the Western Qiang, Lun again submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: When Ma Fang was named general of chariots and cavalry and prepared to lead the western campaign against the Qiang, Lun offered another memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為貴戚可封侯以富之，不當職事以任之。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks that honored kin may be enfeoffed as marquises to enrich them; they should not be given official duties and entrusted with them.
translations.idiomatic: This servant believes imperial in-laws may be ennobled as marquises to keep them wealthy, but they ought not be handed substantive offices.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 繩以法則傷恩，私以親則違憲。

translations.literal: To bind them with law injures grace; to favor with intimacy violates the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Apply the statutes strictly and you wound family feeling; show partiality and you violate the law.

sentences.zh: 伏聞馬防今當西征，臣以太后恩仁，陛下至孝，恐卒有纖介，難為意愛。

translations.literal: I have heard that Ma Fang now ought to campaign west; your servant, considering the empress dowager's kindness and benevolence and Your Majesty's utmost filial piety, fears that suddenly there may be something minute, hard for affectionate intent.
translations.idiomatic: I understand Ma Fang is bound west on campaign. Given the empress dowager's kindness and Your Majesty's deep filial devotion, I fear the slightest friction could strain ties that deserve the utmost care.

sentences.zh: 聞防請杜篤為從事中郎，多賜財帛。

translations.literal: I have heard that Fang requested Du Du as retainer attendant and greatly bestowed wealth and silks.
translations.idiomatic: I hear Fang has appointed Du Du retainer attendant and lavished gifts of silk and cash on him.

sentences.zh: 篤為鄉里所廢，客居美陽，女弟為馬氏妻，恃此交通，在所縣令苦其不法，收係論之。

translations.literal: Du was cast aside by his village and district, lodged as a guest in Meiyang; his younger sister was wife of the Ma clan; relying on this he associated; the county magistrate where he was bitterly suffered his lawlessness, arrested and bound him and sentenced him.
translations.idiomatic: Du was disgraced in his home county and lived as a lodger in Meiyang; his sister married into the Mas, and he traded on that connection until the local magistrate, fed up with his abuses, jailed and convicted him.

sentences.zh: 今來防所，議者咸致疑怪，況乃以為從事，將恐議及朝廷。

translations.literal: Now that he has come to Fang's place, discussers all raise suspicion and wonder; how much more if he is made retainer—I fear discussion will reach the court.
translations.idiomatic: His arrival in Fang's camp already raises eyebrows; appointing him staff officer risks dragging the throne itself into gossip.

sentences.zh: 今宜為選賢能以輔助之，不可復今防自請人，有損事望。

translations.literal: Now it is fitting to select worthy and capable men to assist him; one cannot again let Fang himself request men—there is harm to the affair's prestige.
translations.idiomatic: Choose worthy aides for him now; do not let Fang handpick his own men again—it would damage the expedition's standing.

sentences.zh: 苟有所懷，敢不自聞。

translations.literal: If I harbor something in my breast, how dare I not let it be heard myself.
translations.idiomatic: What I feel I cannot keep from reporting.

sentences.zh: 並不見省用。

translations.literal: (His submissions) were altogether not examined and employed.
translations.idiomatic: None of his advice was adopted.

sentences.zh: 倫雖峭直，然常疾俗吏苛刻。

translations.literal: Although Lun was sternly upright, he constantly hated vulgar officials' harsh severity.
translations.idiomatic: For all his austerity, Lun despised petty officials who ruled by cruelty.

sentences.zh: 及為三公，值帝長者，屢有善政，乃上疏褒稱盛美，因以勸成風德，曰：

translations.literal: When he became one of the three dukes, it happened that the emperor was a senior man and repeatedly had good policies; he then submitted a memorial praising the abundant excellence, and thereby urged completion of custom and virtue, saying:
translations.idiomatic: As one of the three dukes he served under a mature sovereign whose reign brought many good measures; Lun submitted a memorial praising those achievements and urging that they become lasting moral example:

sentences.zh: 及諸馬得罪歸國，而竇氏始貴，倫復上疏曰：

translations.literal: When the various Mas offended, received punishment, and returned to their states, and the Dou clan first became noble, Lun again submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: After the Ma clan fell and was sent back to its fiefs and the Dou family began to rise, Lun addressed the throne again:

sentences.zh: 臣得以空虛之質，當輔弼之任。

translations.literal: Your servant has been able, with empty and hollow substance, to undertake the duty of assistance and support.
translations.idiomatic: Unworthy as I am, I hold a minister's burden.

sentences.zh: 素性駑怯，位尊爵重，抱迫大義，思自策厲，雖遭百死，不敢擇地，又況親遇危言之世哉！

translations.literal: By nature stupid and timid, position noble and rank heavy, embracing and pressed by great righteousness, thinking to spur myself on, although suffering a hundred deaths, I dare not choose the ground—how much more when personally encountering an age of perilous speech!
translations.idiomatic: I am by nature dull and timid, yet honor and duty weigh on me; I mean to steel myself and would face death a hundred times without flinching—especially in an age when plain speech is dangerous.

sentences.zh: 今承百王之敝，人尚文巧，感趨邪路，莫能守正。

translations.literal: Now inheriting the decay of a hundred kings, people esteem literary craftiness, are moved to rush to evil paths, and none can preserve rectitude.
translations.idiomatic: We inherit the slack habits of many reigns: clever words are prized and men veer onto crooked paths—few hold the straight course.

sentences.zh: 伏見虎賁中郎將竇憲，椒房之親，典司禁兵，出入省闥，年盛誌美，卑謙樂善，此誠其好士交結之方。

translations.literal: I humbly see General of the Household for All Purposes Dou Xian, kin of the pepper chambers, in charge of holding the forbidden troops, entering and exiting the palace gates; his years are flourishing and his intent is fine; humble and modest, he delights in goodness—this is truly his method of loving scholars and forming associations.
translations.idiomatic: I observe General Dou Xian of the imperial guard—imperial in-law, commander of the palace corps, moving freely within the inner palace. He is young, ambitious, modest, and eager to do good; it is natural that he should gather clients.

sentences.zh: 然諸出入貴戚者，類多瑕釁禁錮之人，尤少守約安貧之節，士大夫無誌之徒更相販賣，雲集其門。

translations.literal: Yet those who enter and exit among honored kin are for the most part men of blemish and offense who are prohibited and barred, especially lacking the restraint of keeping covenant and resting content in poverty; gentry and grandees without purpose mutually traffic and flock like clouds to his gate.
translations.idiomatic: Yet those who haunt great houses are usually men under stigma or sentence—few are humble scholars content with poverty. Ruthless careerists hawk their services and swarm his door.

sentences.zh: 眾煦飄山，聚蚊成雷，蓋驕佚所從生也。

translations.literal: Many breaths blow away a mountain; gathered mosquitoes become thunder—this is probably where arrogance and dissipation arise.
translations.idiomatic: Many warm breaths can shift a mountain; enough mosquitoes buzz like thunder—that is how arrogance and excess breed.

sentences.zh: 三輔論議者，至雲以貴戚廢錮，當復以貴戚浣濯之，猶解酲當以酒也。

translations.literal: Those who debate in the Three Adjuncts go so far as to say that with honored kin disbarred and barred, one should again use honored kin to wash and cleanse them—it is like curing a hangover with wine.
translations.idiomatic: Wits in the capital joke that if great families fall under ban, other great families must rinse them clean—like curing a hangover with more wine.

sentences.zh: 詖險趣勢之徒，誠不可親近。

translations.literal: Deceitful, perilous, trend-chasing people—truly they cannot be drawn near.
translations.idiomatic: Sycophants who thrive on intrigue must not be indulged.

sentences.zh: 臣愚願陛下中宮嚴敕憲等閉門自守，無妄交通士大夫，防其未萌，慮於無形，令憲永保福祿，君臣交歡，無纖介之隙。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly wishes Your Majesty and the central palace sternly to order Xian and the others to close their gates and guard themselves, not rashly to associate with gentry and grandees; guard against it before it sprouts, consider it while still formless; let Xian forever preserve blessing and salary; ruler and minister mutually rejoice—no minute crack.
translations.idiomatic: I beg Your Majesty and the palace to instruct Dou Xian and his circle to shut their gates, avoid politicking with scholars, and choke trouble before it starts. Forethought now will preserve Dou Xian's fortune and keep ruler and ministers at ease, with never a hair's breadth of suspicion.

sentences.zh: 此臣之至所願也。

translations.literal: This is what your servant most wishes.
translations.idiomatic: That is my deepest hope.

sentences.zh: 倫奉公盡節，言事無所依違。

translations.literal: Lun served the public and exhausted his integrity; in speaking of affairs he had no leaning or deviation.
translations.idiomatic: Lun served the state with absolute integrity and never trimmed his counsel to please.

sentences.zh: 諸子或時諫止，輒叱遣之，吏人奏記及便宜者，亦並封上，其無私若此。

translations.literal: When his sons sometimes remonstrated to stop him, he would shout and send them away; clerks' memorials and communications on convenience, he also sealed and submitted together—his lack of private bias was like this.
translations.idiomatic: When his sons tried to dissuade him he drove them off with a rebuke. Petitions and policy suggestions from subordinates went to the throne sealed—such was his impartiality.

sentences.zh: 性質愨，少文采，在位以貞白稱，時人方之前朝貢禹。

translations.literal: His nature was honest and sincere, little literary polish; in office he was praised for chastity and purity; people of the time compared him to Gong Yu of the former dynasty.
translations.idiomatic: Plain-spoken and little given to rhetoric, he was known in office for incorruption and likened to Gong Yu of the Western Han.

sentences.zh: 然少蘊藉，不修威儀，亦以此見輕。

translations.literal: Yet he lacked reserve and did not cultivate awesome demeanor; on this account he was also slighted.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he lacked polish and paid no heed to dignified bearing, for which some underestimated him.

sentences.zh: 或問倫曰：「公有私乎？

translations.literal: Someone asked Lun, "Does the lord have private bias?
translations.idiomatic: Someone once asked him, "Are you free of private feeling?"

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「昔人有與吾千里馬者，吾雖不受，每三公有所選舉，心不能忘，而亦終不用也。

translations.literal: " He replied, "In the past someone gave me a thousand-li horse; although I did not accept, whenever the three dukes had selections and appointments, my heart could not forget, yet in the end I also did not employ him.
translations.idiomatic: " He said, "Once a man offered me a famous horse. I refused it, but whenever the three dukes held nominations I still thought of him—yet I never put him forward."

sentences.zh: 吾兄子常病，一夜十往，退而安寢；

translations.literal: My elder brother's son was often ill; in one night I went ten times; when I withdrew I slept peacefully;
translations.idiomatic: When my nephew was sick I visited him ten times in a night, then slept soundly afterward;

sentences.zh: 吾子有疾，雖不省視而竟夕不眠。

translations.literal: when my own son had illness, although I did not go to look in on him, I could not sleep the whole night.
translations.idiomatic: when my own son fell ill I might not go to his bedside, yet I lay awake all night.

sentences.zh: 若是者，豈可謂無私乎？

translations.literal: In such a case, how can it be called having no private bias?
translations.idiomatic: Can anyone call that impartiality?"

sentences.zh: 」連以老病上疏乞身。

translations.literal: " Continuously on account of old age and illness he submitted a memorial begging his person.
translations.idiomatic: He repeatedly asked leave to retire on grounds of age and health.

sentences.zh: 元和三年，賜策罷，以二千石奉終其身，加賜錢五十萬，公宅一區。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanhe, he was granted a document and dismissed, with two thousand piculs salary for the rest of his life, additionally granted fifty thousand cash and one public residence.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanhe 3 he received a formal dismissal with a pension at the full governor's rate for life, plus fifty thousand cash and a government residence.

sentences.zh: 後數年卒，時年八十餘，詔賜秘器、衣衾、錢布。

translations.literal: After several years he died, aged over eighty; an edict granted inner coffin, garments and bedding, and cloth.
translations.idiomatic: He died some years later in his eighties; the court sent a lacquered coffin, burial clothes, and cloth for mourning.

sentences.zh: 少子頡嗣，歷桂陽、廬江、南陽太守，所在見稱。

translations.literal: His youngest son Jie inherited; he successively was governor of Guiyang, Lujiang, and Nanyang; wherever he was he was praised.
translations.idiomatic: His youngest son Jie inherited his title and served as governor of Guiyang, Lujiang, and Nanyang, earning praise at every post.

sentences.zh: 順帝之為太子廢也，頡為太中大夫，與太僕來曆等共守闕固爭。

translations.literal: When Emperor Shun as crown prince was deposed, Jie was grand counselor of the palace and together with the coachman Lai Li and others jointly guarded the gate and firmly disputed.
translations.idiomatic: When the heir apparent who became Emperor Shun was cast aside, Jie—then a grand counselor of the palace—joined Coachman Lai Li and others in besieging the palace gates to protest.

sentences.zh: 帝即位，擢為將作大匠，卒官。

translations.literal: When the emperor ascended the throne, he was promoted to court architect and died in office.
translations.idiomatic: When that prince became emperor he promoted Jie to court architect; Jie died in that office.

sentences.zh: 倫曾孫種。

translations.literal: Lun's great-grandson was Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: Lun's great-grandson was Zhong.

sentences.zh: 論曰：第五倫峭核為方，非夫愷悌之士，省其奏議，惇惇歸諸寬厚，將懲苛切之敝使其然乎？

translations.literal: The judgment says: Diwu Lun was sternly exacting as his norm; he was not a man of kind gentleness. Examining his memorials and deliberations, earnestly they return to generous breadth—was it that he wished to correct the harm of harsh severity and made it thus?
translations.idiomatic: Appreciation: Diwu Lun cut a severe figure—no mild Confucian gentleman. Yet read his memorials: again and again he argues for leniency. Perhaps he meant to cure an age addicted to harsh rule.

sentences.zh: 昔人以弦韋為佩，蓋猶此矣。

translations.literal: The ancients wore bowstring and soft leather as ornaments—probably it was still this.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients wore bowstring and soft leather as their pendant—reminders to balance firmness with give. Lun did much the same.

sentences.zh: 然而君子侈不僭上，儉不逼下，豈尊臨千里而與牧圉等庸乎？

translations.literal: Yet the gentleman in extravagance does not transgress above, in frugality does not press below—how could one who nobly overlooks a thousand li be equal in vulgarity to herdsmen and grooms?
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman may live richly without lèse-majesté or plainly without demeaning inferiors; ruling a vast commandery is hardly the same as mucking stables.

sentences.zh: 詎非矯激，則未可以中和言也。

translations.literal: If it is not exaggeration and sharpness, then one cannot yet speak of centrality and harmony.
translations.idiomatic: Unless that stern edge was deliberate counterweight, he could not be called a man of true equipoise.

sentences.zh: 種字興先，少厲誌義，為吏，冠名州郡。

translations.literal: Zhong, styled Xingxian, in youth sharpened his intent on righteousness; as an official, his fame crowned the province and commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong, courtesy name Xingxian, devoted his youth to principle; as soon as he entered office his name led the rolls across his province.

sentences.zh: 永壽中，以司徒掾清詔使冀州，廉察災害，舉奏刺史、二千石以下，所刑免甚眾，棄官奔走者數十人。

translations.literal: In the Yongshou period, as a clerk of the minister of education on a pure edict mission to Ji Province, he inspected and investigated disasters, impeached and memorialized governors and those below two thousand piculs; those sentenced and removed were very many; those who abandoned office and fled were several tens of persons.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongshou he was dispatched to Ji Province as a ministry clerk on an imperial fact-finding mission. Auditing disaster relief, he impeached governors and subordinate officials by the score; many were punished or dismissed, and dozens fled their posts.

sentences.zh: 還，以奉使稱職，拜高密侯相。

translations.literal: Returning, because he fulfilled his mission competently, he was appointed chancellor of the marquisate of Gaomi.
translations.idiomatic: On his return the court judged his mission a success and named him chancellor of the Gaomi marquisate.

sentences.zh: 是時徐、兗二州盜賊群輩，高密在二州之郊，種乃大儲糧稸，勤厲吏士，賊聞皆憚之，桴鼓不鳴，流民歸者，歲中至數千家。

translations.literal: At this time Xu and Yan two commanderies had bandit gangs in groups; Gaomi was on the border of the two commanderies; Zhong then greatly stored grain and reserves, diligently urged officials and soldiers; when bandits heard, all feared them; the drum and mallet did not sound; wandering people who returned within the year reached several thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Xu and Yan were overrun with bandits, and Gaomi lay between them. Zhong stockpiled grain, drilled his troops, and put such fear into the robbers that his district heard no alarm drums; within a year thousands of refugee families came home.

sentences.zh: 以能換為衛相。

translations.literal: Because of ability he was transferred to be chancellor of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: His performance earned him transfer to chancellor of Wei.

sentences.zh: 遷兗州刺史。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be inspector of Yan Province.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to inspector of Yan Province.

sentences.zh: 中常侍單超兄子匡為濟陰太守，負勢貪放，種欲收舉，未知所使。

translations.literal: The attendant at court Shan Chao's elder brother's son Kuang was governor of Jiyin; relying on power he was greedy and unrestrained; Zhong wished to arrest and impeach him, but did not know whom to employ.
translations.idiomatic: Shan Chao's nephew Kuang governed Jiyin and abused his connections with greed and license. Zhong meant to arrest and indict him but had no agent he trusted for the job.

sentences.zh: 會聞從事衛羽素抗厲，乃召羽具告之。

translations.literal: It happened that he heard the retainer Wei Yu was by nature stern and upright, so he summoned Yu and fully informed him.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that his aide Wei Yu had a reputation for fearless integrity, Zhong summoned him and laid out the whole situation.

sentences.zh: 謂曰：「聞公不畏強禦，今欲相委以重事，若何？

translations.literal: He said to him: "I have heard you do not fear the powerful; now I wish to entrust you with a weighty matter—how about it?
translations.idiomatic: He said, "They say you do not flinch from the mighty. I need to charge you with something serious—will you take it on?"

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「願庶幾於一割。

translations.literal: " He replied: "I wish perhaps to be like one cut.
translations.idiomatic: " Yu answered, "Let me prove useful—even a blunt knife gets one good cut."

sentences.zh: 」羽出，遂馳至定陶，閉門收匡賓客親吏四十餘人，六七日中，糾發其臧五六千萬。

translations.literal: " Yu went out, then galloped to Dingtao, closed the gates and seized Kuang's guests and intimate clerks more than forty persons; within six or seven days he investigated and exposed his embezzlement of fifty or sixty million.
translations.idiomatic: Yu rode straight to Dingtao, sealed the yamen, and arrested over forty of Kuang's clients and personal clerks. Within a week he had documented embezzlement on the order of fifty or sixty million cash.

sentences.zh: 種即奏匡，並以劾超。

translations.literal: Zhong immediately memorialized against Kuang, and together impeached Chao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong memorialized against Kuang and joined the indictment of Shan Chao as well.

sentences.zh: 匡窘迫，遣刺客刺羽，羽覺其奸，乃收係客，具得情狀。

translations.literal: Kuang was pressed tight and sent assassins to stab Yu; Yu perceived their treachery, then arrested and bound the guests and fully obtained the circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: Cornered, Kuang sent killers after Yu, who saw through the plot, rounded up the conspirators, and extracted a full confession.

sentences.zh: 州內震栗，朝廷嗟歎之。

translations.literal: Within the province they trembled; the court sighed in admiration at it.
translations.idiomatic: The province was shaken; at court officials spoke of the affair with awe.

sentences.zh: 是時太山賊叔孫無忌等暴橫一境，州郡不能討。

translations.literal: At this time Mount Tai bandits such as Sun Wuji ran riot across a region; the province and commanderies could not suppress them.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile bandits led by Sun Wuji on Mount Tai terrorized the whole area, and local authorities could not bring them to heel.

sentences.zh: 羽說種曰：「中國安寧，忘戰日久，而太山險阻，寇猾不製。

translations.literal: Yu persuaded Zhong, saying: "The central lands are peaceful; war has been forgotten long; yet Mount Tai is rugged and blocked, and the bandits are cunning and unrestrained.
translations.idiomatic: Yu urged Zhong: "The heartland has known peace so long that men have forgotten how to fight, while Mount Tai's terrain favors the brigands—they slip through our fingers."

sentences.zh: 今雖有精兵，難以赴敵，羽請往譬降之。

translations.literal: Now although there are crack troops, it is hard to go against the enemy; Yu requests to go and persuade them to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Even picked troops would struggle in those hills. Let me go talk them into submitting."

sentences.zh: 」種敬諾。

translations.literal: " Zhong respectfully agreed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong agreed.

sentences.zh: 羽乃往，備說禍福，無忌即帥其黨與三千餘人降。

translations.literal: Yu then went and fully explained fortune and disaster; Wuji immediately led his faction and associates more than three thousand persons to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Yu went and laid out the stakes. Wuji promptly brought more than three thousand followers down from the hills.

sentences.zh: 單超積懷忿恨，遂以事陷種，竟坐徙朔方。

translations.literal: Shan Chao harbored accumulated resentment and hatred, then on a pretext trapped Zhong; in the end he was sentenced to banishment to Shuofang.
translations.idiomatic: Shan Chao nursed a grudge and eventually framed Zhong on a charge that sent him into exile on the northern frontier.

sentences.zh: 超外孫董援為朔方太守，稸怒以待之。

translations.literal: Chao's maternal grandson Dong Yuan was governor of Shuofang; he stored anger to await him.
translations.idiomatic: Chao's grandson Dong Yuan governed Shuofang and waited for Zhong with murder in mind.

sentences.zh: 初，種為衛相，以門下掾孫斌賢，善遇之。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Zhong was chancellor of Wei, he considered his clerk Sun Bin worthy and treated him well.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, as chancellor of Wei, Zhong had taken a liking to his clerk Sun Bin and treated him generously.

sentences.zh: 及當徙斥，斌具聞超謀，乃謂其友人同縣閭子直及高密甄子然曰：「蓋盜憎其主，從來舊矣。

translations.literal: When he was about to be banished in disgrace, Bin fully heard of Chao's plot, then said to his friends of the same county Lü Zizhi and of Gaomi Zhen Ziran: "The thief hates his master—it has been so from antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: When exile loomed, Bin learned every detail of Chao's plan and told his friends Lü Zizhi from their county and Zhen Ziran of Gaomi, "A thief hates an honest master—that has always been true.

sentences.zh: 第五使君當投裔土，而單超外屬為彼郡守。

translations.literal: Inspector Diwu is about to be cast to the barbarian lands, while Shan Chao's kinsman by marriage is governor of that commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Our patron Diwu is being shipped to the frontier while Chao's in-law holds that commandery.

sentences.zh: 夫危者易仆，可為寒心。

translations.literal: One in peril easily falls; one may chill the heart.
translations.idiomatic: A man on the edge can be toppled with a breath—it makes your blood run cold."

sentences.zh: 吾今方追使君，庶免其難。

translations.literal: I now am just pursuing the inspector, hoping to spare him difficulty.
translations.idiomatic: I mean to ride after him now and try to save him from that fate.

sentences.zh: 若奉使君以還，將以付子。

translations.literal: If I escort the inspector back, I will deliver him to you.
translations.idiomatic: If I bring him home safely, I will hand him over to you two."

sentences.zh: 」二人曰：「子其行矣，是吾心也。

translations.literal: " The two said: "You go—that is our heart too.
translations.idiomatic: They answered, "Go with our blessing—that is what we want too."

sentences.zh: 」於是斌將俠客晨夜追種，及之於太原，遮險格殺送吏，因下馬與種，斌自步從。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Bin led braves morning and night pursuing Zhong, overtook him at Taiyuan, blocked the narrow place and killed the escort clerks, then dismounted and gave to Zhong; Bin himself followed on foot.
translations.idiomatic: Bin gathered swordsmen and rode night and day until he overtook the escort at Taiyuan, ambushed them in a defile, killed the guards, put Zhong on his own horse, and walked beside him.

sentences.zh: 一日一夜行四百餘里，遂得脫歸。

translations.literal: In one day and one night they went more than four hundred li, and thus were able to escape and return.
translations.idiomatic: They covered over four hundred li in a day and a night and won Zhong his freedom.

sentences.zh: 種匿於閭、甄氏數年，徐州從事臧旻上書訟之曰：

translations.literal: Zhong hid among the Lü and Zhen families several years; Xu Province retainer Zang Min submitted a memorial pleading for him, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Zhong stayed hidden with the Lü and Zhen families for years until Zang Min, an aide in Xu Province, addressed the throne on his behalf:

sentences.zh: 臣聞士有忍死之辱，必有就事之計，故季布屈節於朱家，管仲錯行於召忽。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when a gentleman endures the shame of not dying, he must have a plan to accomplish the task; therefore Ji Bu bent his integrity under the Zhu family, and Guan Zhong went awry from Shao Hu.
translations.idiomatic: I have read that a man who swallows mortal shame does so to serve a larger purpose—Ji Bu humbled himself before the Zhu clan; Guan Zhong broke faith with Shao Hu to save a state.

sentences.zh: 此二臣可以死而不死者，非愛身於須臾，貪命於苟活，隱其智力，顧其權略，庶幸逢時有所為耳。

translations.literal: These two ministers could have died but did not die—not loving their persons for a moment or greedily clinging to life in mere survival, but hiding their intelligence and strength, watching their stratagem, hoping by luck to meet the time and have something to do.
translations.idiomatic: They could have died honorably but chose to live—not from cowardice but to husband their strength until the moment came to act.

sentences.zh: 卒遭高帝之成業，齊桓之興伯，遺其亡逃之行，赦其射鉤之仇，拔於囚虜之中，信其佐國之謀，勳效傳於百世，君臣載於篇籍。

translations.literal: In the end they encountered the High Ancestor's completed enterprise, Duke Huan of Qi's rise to hegemony; they cast aside their fleeing conduct, pardoned the enmity of the arrow on the hook, raised them from among prisoners and captives, trusted their plans to aid the state; merit and effect were transmitted through a hundred generations, ruler and minister were recorded in documents.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu and Duke Huan overlooked flight and old grudges—even the arrow that nearly killed Duke Huan—pulled these men from chains, and trusted their counsel. Their deeds outlive them in the histories.

sentences.zh: 假令二主紀過於纖介，則此二臣同死於犬馬，沉名於溝壑，當何由得申其補過之功，建其奇奧之術乎？

translations.literal: Suppose the two lords had recorded faults at the minute—then these two ministers would have died together like dogs and horses, sunk their names in ditches and gullies; how would they have been able to extend their merit of repairing faults or establish their marvelous subtle arts?
translations.idiomatic: Had those rulers punished petty faults, both men would have rotted unremembered in ditches—what chance then to redeem themselves or serve with genius?

sentences.zh: 伏見故兗州刺史第五種，傑然自建，在鄉曲無苞苴之嫌，步朝堂無擇言之闕，天性疾惡，公方不曲，故論者說清高以種為上，序直士以種為首。

translations.literal: I humbly see the former inspector of Yan Province Diwu Zhong, outstandingly self-established; in village lanes he had no suspicion of gift bundles; on the court hall he had no lack of selective speech; by nature he hated evil, was public and square and not crooked; therefore debaters ranked purity and height with Zhong highest, and ordered straight gentlemen with Zhong first.
translations.idiomatic: Your former Yan inspector Diwu Zhong held himself apart: no whiff of bribery in his district, no careless word at court. He loathed wickedness by instinct and would not bend for favor. Critics ranked him first among the incorrupt and the plain-spoken.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之義，選人所長，棄其所短，錄其小善，除其大過。

translations.literal: The meaning of the Spring and Autumn is to select people's strengths, discard their shortcomings, record their small goodnesses, remove their great faults.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn teaches us to prize what is strong in a man, forgive weakness, credit small virtue, and overlook great fault when justice demands.

sentences.zh: 種所坐以盜賊公負，筋力未就，罪至征徙，非有大惡。

translations.literal: What Zhong sat guilty for was due to bandits and public burden; sinew and strength had not been accomplished; the crime reached summons and banishment—there was no great evil.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong's case arose from banditry and official duty left undone—the sentence was exile, not some monstrous crime.

sentences.zh: 昔虞舜事親，大杖則走。

translations.literal: Formerly Shun of Yu served his kin; with a great staff he would run.
translations.idiomatic: Even Shun fled the beating when the stick was too heavy.

sentences.zh: 故種逃亡，苟全性命，冀有朱家之路，以顯季布之會，願陛下無遺須臾之恩，令種有持忠入地之恨。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhong fled and barely preserved life and fate, hoping there might be a Zhu family's road, to manifest Ji Bu's meeting; I wish Your Majesty not to withhold a moment's grace, causing Zhong to hold the regret of bearing loyalty into the ground.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong ran to save his skin, hoping for the sort of rescue Zhu Jia gave Ji Bu. Do not withhold the smallest mercy and leave him to die loyal but unsung.

sentences.zh: 會赦出，卒於家。

translations.literal: It happened that he was released by amnesty and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty freed him; he died at home.

sentences.zh: 鍾離意

translations.literal: Zhongli Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongli Yi

sentences.zh: 鍾離意字子阿，會稽山陰人也。

translations.literal: Zhongli Yi, styled Zi'e, was a man of Shanyin in Kuaiji.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongli Yi, courtesy name Zi'e, came from Shanyin in Kuaiji commandery.

sentences.zh: 少為郡督郵。

translations.literal: In youth he was provincial courier.
translations.idiomatic: In his youth he served as the commandery's courier inspector.

sentences.zh: 時部縣亭長有受人酒禮者，府下記案考之。

translations.literal: At that time in the division counties a post chief had accepted someone's wine gift; the prefecture issued a note to investigate and examine him.
translations.idiomatic: A village post chief in his circuit had accepted wine as a bribe; the prefecture ordered an inquiry.

sentences.zh: 意封還記，入言於太守曰：「《春秋》先內後外，《詩》云『刑於寡妻，以禦於家邦』，明政化之本，由近及遠。

translations.literal: Yi sealed and returned the note, entered and spoke to the governor, saying: "The Spring and Autumn puts inner first and outer after; the Classic of Poetry says 'Model yourself on your wife to regulate family and state'—it clarifies the root of governance and transformation, from near reaching far.
translations.idiomatic: Yi sent the warrant back unopened and told the governor, "The Spring and Autumn teaches us to set our own house in order before looking outward. The Poetry says, 'Begin with your wife, then rule kin and state.' Reform starts close to home."

sentences.zh: 今宜先清府內，且闊略遠縣細微之愆。

translations.literal: Now it is fitting first to clear the prefecture interior, and for the moment broadly overlook distant counties' minute faults.
translations.idiomatic: Clean the yamen first and leave remote counties' petty slips for later."

sentences.zh: 」太守甚賢之，遂任以縣事。

translations.literal: " The governor greatly deemed him worthy and thereupon entrusted him with county affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The governor was impressed and put him in charge of county business.

sentences.zh: 建武十四年，會稽大疫，死者萬數，意獨身自隱親，經給醫藥，所部多蒙全濟。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year of Jianwu, Kuaiji had a great epidemic; the dead were in myriads; Yi alone personally attended kin, supplied medicine, and those under his jurisdiction mostly received full relief.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 14 a plague struck Kuaiji and claimed tens of thousands. Yi went himself from house to house among his people with medicines, and most of those under his care survived.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，再遷，辟大司徒侯霸府。

translations.literal: He was recommended as filial and incorrupt, twice was promoted, and was summoned to Grand Minister of Education Hou Ba's office.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended filial and incorrupt, promoted twice, he was recruited into Grand Minister Hou Ba's administration.

sentences.zh: 詔部送徒詣河內，時冬寒，徒病不能行。

translations.literal: An edict ordered his division to escort convicts to Henei; at the time it was winter cold, and the convicts were ill and could not walk.
translations.idiomatic: The court ordered his unit to march convicts to Henei, but winter cold laid them low and they could not march.

sentences.zh: 路過弘農，意輒移屬縣使作徒衣，縣不得已與之，而上書言狀，意亦具以聞。

translations.literal: Passing through Hongnong, Yi then shifted order to subordinate counties to make convict clothing; the counties had no choice but to give it, and submitted a memorial stating the situation; Yi also fully reported it.
translations.idiomatic: At Hongnong he ordered each county along the route to sew winter clothes for the men. The counties complied and memorialized; Yi reported the same to the throne.

sentences.zh: 光武得奏，以視霸，曰：「君所使掾何乃仁於用心？

translations.literal: Guangwu received the memorial and showed it to Ba, saying: "What clerk you employ—how benevolent in intent?
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu showed the memorial to Hou Ba and said, "Where did you find a clerk with such a humane heart?"

sentences.zh: 誠良吏也！

translations.literal: Truly a good official!
translations.idiomatic: This is the real thing—a true official."

sentences.zh: 」意遂於道解徒桎梏，恣所欲過，與克期俱至，無或違者。

translations.literal: " Yi then on the road released the convicts' fetters and cangues, let them pass as they wished, and agreed on a date to arrive together—none violated it.
translations.idiomatic: Yi removed the manacles on the road, let the men travel at their own pace, and set a rendezvous date in Henei. Everyone arrived on time.

sentences.zh: 還，以病免。

translations.literal: Returning, on account of illness he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward he resigned on grounds of ill health.

sentences.zh: 後除瑕丘令。

translations.literal: Later he was appointed magistrate of Xiaqiu.
translations.idiomatic: He was later named magistrate of Xiaqiu.

sentences.zh: 吏有檀建者，盜竊縣內，意屏人問狀，建叩頭服罪，不忍加刑，遣令長休。

translations.literal: There was a clerk Tan Jian who stole within the county; Yi dismissed others and asked the situation; Jian knocked his head and confessed guilt; Yi could not bear to apply punishment and sent him to take long leave.
translations.idiomatic: A clerk named Tan Jian had stolen public funds. Yi questioned him alone; Tan confessed, and Yi, unable to bring himself to punish him, sent him away on prolonged leave.

sentences.zh: 建父聞之，為建設酒，謂曰：「吾聞無道之君以刃殘人，有道之君以義行誅。

translations.literal: Jian's father heard it, set up wine for Jian, and said to him: "I have heard that a ruler without the Way mutilates people with blades; a ruler with the Way carries out execution by righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Jian's father set out wine and said, "I have read that a wicked lord kills with the sword; a good lord punishes through justice.

sentences.zh: 子罪，命也。

translations.literal: Son's crime is fate.
translations.idiomatic: Your guilt is your fate."

sentences.zh: 」遂令建進藥而死。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he ordered Jian to ingest poison and die.
translations.idiomatic: He made Jian drink poison and die.

sentences.zh: 二十五年，遷堂邑令。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fifth year he was transferred to be magistrate of Tangyi.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fifth year of the reign he became magistrate of Tangyi.

sentences.zh: 縣人防廣為父報仇，係獄，其母病死，廣哭泣不食。

translations.literal: County person Fang Guang avenged his father, was bound in prison; his mother died of illness; Guang wept and did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: Fang Guang of the county lay in jail for avenging his father. When his mother died he wept and refused food.

sentences.zh: 意憐傷之，乃聽廣歸家，使得殯斂。

translations.literal: Yi pitied and grieved for him, then allowed Guang to return home and enabled him to bury and dress the dead.
translations.idiomatic: Yi pitied him and let him go home to arrange his mother's funeral.

sentences.zh: 丞掾皆爭，意曰：「罪自我歸，義不累下。

translations.literal: The assistant magistrate and clerks all disputed; Yi said: "Guilt from me returns; righteousness does not drag down those below.
translations.idiomatic: His deputies protested. Yi said, "Blame will fall on me alone—I will not let principle hurt subordinates."

sentences.zh: 」遂遣之。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he sent him.
translations.idiomatic: He let Guang go.

sentences.zh: 廣斂母訖，果還入獄。

translations.literal: Guang finished burying his mother and indeed returned and entered prison.
translations.idiomatic: When the burial was done, Guang walked back into jail as promised.

sentences.zh: 意密以狀聞，廣竟得以減死論。

translations.literal: Yi secretly reported the situation on hearing; Guang in the end was able to obtain judgment reducing death.
translations.idiomatic: Yi quietly memorialized the facts, and Guang's sentence was commuted from death.

sentences.zh: 顯宗即位，徵為尚書。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he was summoned as secretary.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ming took the throne he summoned Yi to the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 時交阯太守張恢，坐臧千金，徵還伏法，以資物簿入大司農，詔班賜群臣。

translations.literal: At that time Jiaozhi governor Zhang Hui, sitting guilty of embezzling a thousand in gold, was summoned back and submitted to law; the goods ledger entered the grand minister of agriculture; an edict distributed and bestowed on the various ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The governor of Jiaozhi, Zhang Hui, had been executed for embezzling a fortune; his confiscated goods went to the ministry of finance, and the emperor ordered them shared among officials.

sentences.zh: 意得珠璣，悉以委地而不拜賜。

translations.literal: Yi received pearls and jades; he entirely threw them to the ground and did not bow to accept the gift.
translations.idiomatic: Yi was allotted pearls and jade but dropped them on the floor and refused to accept the grant.

sentences.zh: 帝怪而問其故。

translations.literal: The emperor wondered and asked his reason.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked why.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣聞孔子忍渴於盜泉之水，曾參回車於勝母之閭，惡其名也。

translations.literal: He replied: "Your servant has heard Confucius endured thirst at Stolen Spring's waters; Zeng Shen turned his cart at Victory Mother's lane—he hated their names.
translations.idiomatic: Yi answered, "Confucius would not drink from Thieves' Spring; Zeng Shen would not enter Victory Mother Lane—the names were foul.

sentences.zh: 此臧穢之寶，誠不敢拜。

translations.literal: These are treasures of filthy embezzlement; truly I dare not bow.
translations.idiomatic: These gems are stained by corruption—I cannot take them in good conscience."

sentences.zh: 」帝嗟歎曰：「清乎尚書之言！

translations.literal: " The emperor sighed and said: "Pure indeed are the secretary's words!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "How pure—what the secretary says!"

sentences.zh: 」乃更以庫錢三十萬賜意。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he changed to granting Yi three hundred thousand cash from the treasury.
translations.idiomatic: He gave Yi three hundred thousand cash from the treasury instead.

sentences.zh: 轉為尚書僕射。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be vice director of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to vice director of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 車駕數幸廣成苑，意以為從禽廢政，常當年陣諫般樂遊田之事，天子即時還宮。

translations.literal: The imperial carriage several times visited Guangcheng Park; Yi thought following hunting wasted government; he constantly faced the year's formation remonstrating about lavish pleasure and roaming hunt affairs; the Son of Heaven immediately returned to the palace.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor often hunted at Guangcheng Park. Yi believed sport was distracting him from rule and repeatedly planted himself in the emperor's path to protest extravagant outings; each time the sovereign turned back to the palace.

sentences.zh: 永平三年夏旱，而大起北宮，意詣闕免冤上疏曰：

translations.literal: In the third year of Yongping, summer was drought, yet they greatly raised the Northern Palace; Yi went to the palace gate, removed his cap, and submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 3 drought gripped the land while labor gangs threw up the Northern Palace. Yi appeared at the palace gate bareheaded to remonstrate and memorialized:

sentences.zh: 伏見陛下以天時小旱，憂念元元，降避正殿，躬自克責，而比日密雲，遂無大潤，豈政有未得應天心者邪？

translations.literal: I humbly see Your Majesty because heaven's season had slight drought, worried and thought on the common folk, descended and avoided the main hall, personally blamed himself, yet successive days dense clouds, yet no great moisture—could government have something not obtaining correspondence with heaven's heart?
translations.idiomatic: You have worried for the people in this drought, moved out of the main hall, and blamed yourself—yet day after day the clouds gather without rain. Does something in our governance still fail heaven?

sentences.zh: 昔成湯遭旱，以六事自責曰：「政不節邪？

translations.literal: Formerly Cheng Tang encountered drought and blamed himself with six matters, saying: "Is government not restrained?
translations.idiomatic: When Cheng Tang faced drought he asked himself six questions: "Have I misruled?

sentences.zh: 使人疾邪？

translations.literal: Do I make people sick?
translations.idiomatic: Have I burdened the people?

sentences.zh: 宮室榮邪？

translations.literal: Are palace chambers glorious?
translations.idiomatic: Are my palaces too grand?

sentences.zh: 女謁盛邪？

translations.literal: Are women's requests abundant?
translations.idiomatic: Does patronage through the women's quarters run unchecked?

sentences.zh: 苞苴行邪？

translations.literal: Do gift bundles circulate?
translations.idiomatic: Is bribery rife?

sentences.zh: 讒夫昌邪？

translations.literal: Are slanderers flourishing?
translations.idiomatic: Do slanderers prosper?"

sentences.zh: 」竊見北宮大作，人失農時，此所謂宮室榮也。

translations.literal: " I privately see the Northern Palace greatly built; people lose farming season—this is what is called palace chambers glorious.
translations.idiomatic: " I see the Northern Palace rising on a vast scale while farmers miss the seasons—that is 'palaces too grand.'"

sentences.zh: 自古非苦宮室小狹，但患人不安寧。

translations.literal: From antiquity it has not been suffering that palace chambers are small and narrow—only worrying that people are not peaceful.
translations.idiomatic: History does not fault rulers for small halls but for unrest among the people.

sentences.zh: 宜且罷止，以應天心。

translations.literal: It is fitting for the moment to stop and cease, to correspond with heaven's heart.
translations.idiomatic: Suspend the work now and answer heaven.

sentences.zh: 臣意以匹夫之才，無有行能，久食重祿，擢備近臣，比受厚賜，喜懼相並，不勝愚戇征營，罪當萬死！

translations.literal: Your servant Yi with a common man's talent, having no conduct or ability, long eating heavy salary, promoted and prepared as intimate minister, recently receiving thick gifts, joy and fear side by side, cannot bear foolish bluntness and anxious fluttering—crime deserves ten thousand deaths!
translations.idiomatic: I am a man of ordinary gifts yet I draw a heavy stipend and stand close to the throne; your recent bounty fills me with gratitude and fear. I tremble at my blunt words—I deserve death a thousand times over."

sentences.zh: 帝策詔報曰：「湯引六事，咎在一人。

translations.literal: The emperor issued an edict in reply, saying: "Tang cited six matters; fault is in one person.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied by edict: "Tang's six questions found the fault in himself alone.

sentences.zh: 其冠履，勿謝。

translations.literal: As for caps and shoes, do not apologize.
translations.idiomatic: Keep your cap and shoes—I absolve your gesture of shame."

sentences.zh: 比上天降旱，密雲數會，朕戚然慚懼，思獲嘉應，故分布禱請，窺候風雲，北祈明堂，南設雩場。

translations.literal: Recently upper heaven sent drought; dense clouds several times gathered; I sorrowfully felt shame and fear, thinking to obtain auspicious response; therefore I spread out prayers and requests, watched and waited wind and clouds; north prayed at the Bright Hall; south set up rain-altar.
translations.idiomatic: These drought years with lowering clouds but no rain have shamed and frightened me. I have prayed everywhere—reading the skies, sacrificing north at the Bright Hall and south at the rain altar—seeking heaven's favor.

sentences.zh: 今又敕大匠止作諸宮，減省不急，庶消災譴。

translations.literal: Now again I order the grand craftsman to stop work on the various palaces, reduce and cut what is not urgent, hoping to dispel disaster and reproach.
translations.idiomatic: I have now ordered the superintendent of works to halt palace projects and cut every nonessential expense, hoping to lift this scourge.

sentences.zh: 」詔因謝公卿百僚，遂應時澍雨焉。

translations.literal: " The edict thereby apologized to dukes, ministers, and hundred officials, and then timely soaking rain fell.
translations.idiomatic: " The court apologized to the bureaucracy, and timely rain followed.

sentences.zh: 時，詔賜降胡子縑，尚書案事，誤以十為百。

translations.literal: At that time an edict granted surrendered Hu barbarians silk; the secretariat examined the affair and mistakenly took ten as one hundred.
translations.idiomatic: An edict awarded silk to surrendered northern tribesmen; the secretariat bungled the paperwork and multiplied ten rolls into one hundred.

sentences.zh: 帝見司農上簿，大怒，召郎，將笞之。

translations.literal: The emperor saw the minister of agriculture submit the ledger and was greatly angry; he summoned the gentleman and was about to cane him.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor saw the agriculture ministry's ledger he exploded and ordered the clerk responsible dragged in for a beating.

sentences.zh: 意因入叩頭曰：「過誤之失，常人所容。

translations.literal: Yi thereupon entered and knocked his head, saying: "Errors of mistake—ordinary people tolerate them.
translations.idiomatic: Yi threw himself forward and said, "Honest mistakes happen every day.

sentences.zh: 若以懈慢為愆，則臣位大，罪重，郎位小，罪輕，咎皆在臣，臣當先坐。

translations.literal: If by slack negligence one is faulted, then your servant's position is great, guilt heavy; the gentleman's position is small, guilt light; blame is all in your servant—your servant ought first to sit punishment.
translations.idiomatic: If this is negligence, then fault lies heavier on me than on him—I rank higher and should be punished first.

sentences.zh: 」乃解衣就格。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he stripped his garment and approached the frame.
translations.idiomatic: " He stripped to the waist and lay on the bench for the cane.

sentences.zh: 帝意解，使復冠而貰郎。

translations.literal: The emperor's intent dissolved; he ordered him to put on cap again and pardon the gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor relented, let Yi dress, and spared the clerk.

sentences.zh: 帝性褊察，好以耳目隱發為明，故公卿大臣數被詆毀，近臣尚書以下至見提拽。

translations.literal: The emperor's nature was narrow and scrutinizing; he loved to take ears and eyes' hidden revelations as clarity; therefore dukes and ministers often suffered slander; intimate ministers from secretary downward even saw dragging and pulling.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was suspicious and sharp-eyed, proud of catching secrets through informers. Great ministers were constantly humiliated; even secretaries were hauled about by the collar.

sentences.zh: 嘗以事怒郎藥崧，以杖撞之。

translations.literal: Once on account of affairs he was angry at Gentleman Yao Song and struck him with a staff.
translations.idiomatic: Once he lost his temper with the gentleman Yao Song and beat him with a stick.

sentences.zh: 崧走入床下，帝怒甚，疾言曰：「郎出！

translations.literal: Song ran under the bed; the emperor was very angry and spoke sharply, saying: "Gentleman out!
translations.idiomatic: Song scrambled under the couch. The emperor shouted, "Come out!

sentences.zh: 郎出！

translations.literal: Gentleman out!
translations.idiomatic: Come out!"

sentences.zh: 」崧曰：「天子穆穆，諸侯煌煌。

translations.literal: " Song said: "The Son of Heaven is august; the feudal lords are splendid.
translations.idiomatic: From beneath the bed Song said, "The Son of Heaven should be solemn; the feudal lords resplendent.

sentences.zh: 未聞入君自起撞郎。

translations.literal: I have not heard of a ruler personally rising to strike a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: I have never heard of a sovereign who climbs down to thrash his own gentleman."

sentences.zh: 」帝赦之。

translations.literal: " The emperor pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor let him go.

sentences.zh: 朝廷莫不悚栗，爭為嚴切，以避誅責；

translations.literal: At court none did not tremble; they vied to be harsh and severe to avoid blame for execution.
translations.idiomatic: The whole court walked on eggshells, each man trying to seem sterner than the next to escape punishment.

sentences.zh: 惟意獨敢諫爭，數封還詔書，臣下過失輒救解之。

translations.literal: Only Yi alone dared remonstrate and dispute; several times he sealed and returned edicts; when subordinates had faults he always rescued and explained them.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhongli Yi dared speak plainly—he repeatedly returned unacceptable edicts unopened and shielded officials who erred.

sentences.zh: 會連有變異，意復上疏曰：

translations.literal: It happened that there were successive anomalies; Yi again submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: When strange portents came one after another, Yi addressed the throne again:

sentences.zh: 伏惟陛下躬行孝道，修明經術，郊祀天地，畏敬鬼神，憂恤黎元，勞心不怠。

translations.literal: I humbly consider that Your Majesty personally practices filial piety, cultivates and clarifies classic learning, sacrifices at the suburbs to heaven and earth, fears and reveres ghosts and spirits, worries and pities the black-haired people, toils in mind without slackening.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty lives filial piety, honors the classics, sacrifices to heaven and earth, stands in awe of the spirits, and labors without rest for the common people.

sentences.zh: 而天氣未和，日月不明，水泉湧溢，寒暑違節者，咎在群臣不能宣化理職，而以苛刻為俗。

translations.literal: Yet weather is not harmonious, sun and moon are not bright, springs and streams bubble over, cold and heat violate the seasons—the fault lies in the throng of ministers being unable to proclaim transformation and order duty, and taking harsh severity as custom.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the seasons fail, the lamps of sun and moon dim, rivers overrun their banks, and cold and heat reverse themselves—the ministers have failed to spread your virtue and govern rightly; harshness has become habit.

sentences.zh: 吏殺良人，繼踵不絕。

translations.literal: Officials kill good people; following heels without cease.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrates kill the innocent one after another without pause.

sentences.zh: 百官無相親之心，吏人無雍雍之志。

translations.literal: The hundred offices have no hearts of mutual kindness; clerks and people have no harmonious intent.
translations.idiomatic: High officials share no mutual goodwill; clerks and commoners live without harmony.

sentences.zh: 至於骨肉相殘，毒害彌深，感逆和氣，以致天災。

translations.literal: As for flesh and bone harming each other, poison harm ever deeper, moving and reversing harmonious qi, thereby bringing heavenly disaster.
translations.idiomatic: Kin turn on kin; malice deepens; the gentle breath of harmony is broken—and heaven sends calamity.

sentences.zh: 百姓可以德勝，難以力服。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames can be overcome by virtue; it is hard to subdue them by force.
translations.idiomatic: The people yield to virtue, not to brute force.

sentences.zh: 先王要道，民用和睦，故能致天下和平，災害不生，禍亂不作。

translations.literal: The former kings' essential way—the people used harmony and concord; therefore they could bring about all-under-heaven peace; disasters did not arise; calamity and rebellion did not occur.
translations.idiomatic: The ancient kings taught men to live at peace with one another—so the realm stayed quiet, free of plague and rebellion.

sentences.zh: 《鹿鳴》之詩必言宴樂者，以人神之心洽，然後天氣和也。

translations.literal: The poem "Deer Call" necessarily speaks of feast and music because human and spirit hearts are in harmony, and afterward heaven's qi is harmonious.
translations.idiomatic: The "Deer Call" ode praises feast and music because when men and spirits are at one, the weather itself turns kind.

sentences.zh: 願陛下垂聖德，揆萬機，詔有司，慎人命，緩刑罰，順時氣，以調陰陽，垂之無極。

translations.literal: I wish Your Majesty to extend sagely virtue, manage the myriad mechanisms, order the responsible offices, be careful with human lives, relax punishments, comply with seasonal qi, to harmonize yin and yang—extend it without limit.
translations.idiomatic: Extend your sacred virtue, weigh every policy, charge the agencies to spare lives, ease punishments, and move with the seasons so yin and yang stay balanced—may this never end.

sentences.zh: 帝雖不能用，然知其至誠。

translations.literal: Although the emperor could not employ it, yet he knew his utmost sincerity.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor did not adopt every proposal but recognized Yi's absolute sincerity.

sentences.zh: 亦以此故不得久留，出為魯相。

translations.literal: Also for this reason he could not long remain and went out as chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: For that reason he could not long remain at court and was posted chancellor of Lu.

sentences.zh: 後德陽殿成，百官大會。

translations.literal: Later when Deyang Hall was completed, the hundred officials held a great assembly.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Deyang Hall was finished, the court gathered for the dedication.

sentences.zh: 帝思意言，謂公卿曰：「鍾離尚書若在，此殿不立。」

translations.literal: The emperor thought on Yi's words and said to dukes and ministers: "If Secretary Zhongli were still here, this hall would not have been raised."
translations.idiomatic: Remembering Yi's warnings, the emperor told his ministers, "If Secretary Zhongli were alive, this hall would never have been built."

sentences.zh: 意視事五年，以愛利為化，人多殷富。

translations.literal: Yi held office five years; taking love of benefit as transformation, people mostly became wealthy.
translations.idiomatic: Yi governed Lu for five years with kindness and restraint; the people grew prosperous.

sentences.zh: 以久病卒官。

translations.literal: On account of long illness he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office after a long illness.

sentences.zh: 遺言上書陳升平之世，難以急化，宜少寬假。

translations.literal: His dying words submitted a memorial stating that in an age of rising peace, it is hard to transform by urgency—it is fitting slightly to broaden and indulgence.
translations.idiomatic: His final memorial urged that in peaceful times harsh reform avails little—the court should ease its grip.

sentences.zh: 帝感傷其意，下詔嗟歎，賜錢二十萬。

translations.literal: The emperor was moved and grieved at his intent, issued an edict sighing in admiration, and granted two hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: Touched, the emperor issued an edict of praise and sent two hundred thousand cash to his house.

sentences.zh: 藥崧者，河內人，天性樸忠。

translations.literal: Yao Song was a man of Henei; by nature he was plain and loyal.
translations.idiomatic: Yao Song of Henei was plainspoken and loyal by nature.

sentences.zh: 家貧為郎，常獨直臺上，無被，枕杫，食糟糠。

translations.literal: His family was poor; as gentleman he often alone stood duty on the terrace; he had no quilt, used a stool as pillow, ate lees and bran.
translations.idiomatic: Poor, he served as a palace gentleman and often pulled night duty alone without bedding—just a wooden stool for a pillow—living on chaff and husks.

sentences.zh: 帝每夜入臺，輒見崧，問其故，甚嘉之，自此詔太官賜尚書以下朝夕餐，給帷被阜袍，及侍史二人。

translations.literal: Whenever the emperor entered the terrace by night, he always saw Song; asked his reason, greatly praised him; from this edicted the grand provisioner to grant secretaries downward morning and evening meals, supply curtains, quilts, thick robes, and two attendant clerks.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor often found him there on night rounds, heard his story, and commended him. After that the court kitchen fed the secretariat morning and evening, issued bedding and winter robes, and assigned two clerks to assist.

sentences.zh: 崧官至南陽太守。

translations.literal: Song's office reached governor of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 宋均字叔癢，南陽安眾人也。

translations.literal: Song Jun, styled Shuyang, was a man of Anzhong in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Song Jun, courtesy name Shuyang, came from Anzhong in Nanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: 父伯，建武初為五官中郎將。

translations.literal: His father Bo, at the beginning of Jianwu, was general of the household for all purposes.
translations.idiomatic: His father Song Bo served as general of the household at the start of the Jianwu era.

sentences.zh: 均以父任為郎，時年十五，好經書，每休沐日，輒受業博士，通《詩》、《禮》，善論難。

translations.literal: Jun by father's privilege became gentleman; at the time age fifteen; he loved classics; every rest-and-bathe day, always received instruction from erudits; comprehended the Poetry and Ritual; was good at debate and questioning.
translations.idiomatic: Thanks to his father's rank he became a gentleman at fifteen. He loved the classics, studied under court scholars on every rest day, mastered the Poetry and the Ritual, and excelled at disputation.

sentences.zh: 至二十餘，調補辰陽長。

translations.literal: When he reached twenty-something, he was transferred and appointed magistrate of Chenyang.
translations.idiomatic: In his twenties he was posted magistrate of Chenyang.

sentences.zh: 其俗少學者而信巫鬼，均為立學校，禁絕淫祀，人皆安之。

translations.literal: Their custom had few scholars and believed in shamans and ghosts; Jun for them established schools, prohibited and cut off excessive sacrifices; people all were secure in it.
translations.idiomatic: The people had few schools and trusted witchcraft; Jun founded academies, stamped out illegitimate cults, and the populace accepted his rule.

sentences.zh: 以祖母喪去官，客授潁川。

translations.literal: On account of grandmother's bereavement he left office and as guest taught in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned to mourn his grandmother, then taught privately in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 後為謁者。

translations.literal: Later he became herald.
translations.idiomatic: He later served as a court herald.

sentences.zh: 會武陵蠻反，圍武威將軍劉尚，詔使均乘傳發江夏奔命三千人往救之。

translations.literal: It happened that Wuling Man rebelled and besieged General Who Displays Might Liu Shang; an edict ordered Jun to ride relay post and dispatch Jiangxia emergency-response three thousand men to go rescue him.
translations.idiomatic: When Wuling tribes rose and besieged General Liu Shang, the court ordered Song Jun to take the post relays and raise three thousand emergency troops from Jiangxia for the relief march.

sentences.zh: 既至而尚已沒。

translations.literal: When they arrived, Shang had already perished.
translations.idiomatic: They arrived too late—Liu Shang was already dead.

sentences.zh: 會伏波將軍馬援至，詔因令均監軍，與諸將俱進，賊拒厄不得前。

translations.literal: It happened that General Who Calms the Waves Ma Yuan arrived; an edict thereupon ordered Jun to supervise the army and advance together with the various generals; bandits blocked narrows and could not go forward.
translations.idiomatic: General Ma Yuan reached the front soon after, and an edict named Song Jun army supervisor. The combined force advanced until the tribes held the defiles and stalled the campaign.

sentences.zh: 及馬援卒於師，軍士多溫濕疾病，死者太半。

translations.literal: When Ma Yuan died in the army, soldiers mostly had warm-damp diseases; the dead were more than half.
translations.idiomatic: When Ma Yuan died on campaign, damp heat felled the army until more than half the men were lost.

sentences.zh: 均慮軍遂不反，乃與諸將議曰：「今道遠士病，不可以戰，欲權承製降之何如？

translations.literal: Jun feared the army would then not return, then discussed with the various generals, saying: "Now the road is far and soldiers are sick; we cannot fight; I wish temporarily to assume imperial mandate to surrender them—how about it?
translations.idiomatic: Fearing the army might never get home, Song Jun asked the generals, "The march is long and the men are sick—we cannot fight. What if I assume emergency authority and accept the tribes' surrender?"

sentences.zh: 」諸將皆伏地莫敢應。

translations.literal: " The various generals all prostrated on the ground and none dared respond.
translations.idiomatic: The generals knelt in silence—no one dared answer.

sentences.zh: 均曰：「夫忠臣出竟，有可以安國家，專之可也。

translations.literal: Jun said: "When a loyal minister goes beyond the border, if there is means to secure the state, to act on one's own is permissible.
translations.idiomatic: Jun said, "A loyal minister beyond the frontier may act on his own when the state's safety requires it."

sentences.zh: 」乃矯製調伏波司馬呂種守沅陵長，命種奉詔書入虜營，告以恩信，因勒兵隨其後。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he falsely ordered Pacifying Waves major Lü Zhong to guard as magistrate of Yuanling, ordered Zhong to carry the edict document into the barbarian camp, announce grace and good faith, and thereby marshall troops following behind.
translations.idiomatic: He forged orders naming Ma Yuan's major Lü Zhong magistrate of Yuanling and sent him into the enemy camp with a proclamation of mercy while Song Jun massed troops behind him.

sentences.zh: 蠻夷震怖，即共斬其大帥而降，於是入賊營，散其眾，遣歸本郡，為置長吏而還。
